name: Deterministic Baseline And Version Freeze Policy
description: Propose a governance rule set for deterministic baseline and version freeze policy.
related_rules: All core evaluation paths MUST be deterministic for fixed inputs and configuration in the deterministic writing architecture.
All production evaluation paths must be deterministic for fixed inputs, versions, and configuration.
Evaluator prompts, thresholds, and model versions must be frozen for any baseline used in promotion decisions.
External dependency changes, including evaluator or model-version changes, require fresh benchmark replay before promotion.
Failure taxonomy changes must be versioned and may not silently rewrite historical labels.
Style fingerprints must be versioned and frozen between releases unless changed through governance.
Systemic-versus-one-off failure classification must use deterministic clustering or deterministic feature analytics in primary workflows.
related_validators: Apply template echo detector to sample batches for each major prompt or fine-tune version, adjusting thresholds using benchmark fixtures.
Cluster failure-ledger entries periodically to identify systemic spikes by fixture, genre, style, failure type, and model version.
Configure evaluators (LLM-judges, discriminators, metrics) to run with greedy decoding and fixed seeds to ensure deterministic behavior.
Convert approved representative failures into versioned benchmark fixtures and record failure-to-fixture lineage.
For any change to prompts, rubrics, or fixtures, record a new version identifier and run prompt sensitivity and stability checks.
Freeze evaluator prompts, thresholds, and configs per release so score comparisons remain historically meaningful.
Load continuity state snapshots before generation or refinement and emit deterministic `state_diff` artifacts after each segment.
Maintain `failure_taxonomy.json` as a controlled vocabulary with explicit versioning.
Write every failure event to `failure_log.jsonl` with fixture id, pipeline stage, version metadata, evaluator outputs, and resolution state.
